The intended message 'Give me a minute' translates to 'Guv bu u budud'. One misinterpretation involves the word 'bu', which can be from 'me' or 'pie' (p->b, ie->uu/u). The phrase 'Give pie a minute' sounds very similar, leading a friend to bring a pie, which is a fruit-based food fitting the humorous context.

The intended message 'Give me a minute' ('Guv bu u budud') is homophonous with 'Give me a mint' in Duw Ulbubud, because 'minute' (m-i-n-u-t-e) and 'mint' (m-i-n-t) both translate to 'budud'. This leads the other friend to bring the vegetable, mint.

The new name 'Zduvvu-Duzu Dulg' is a pun based on its sound, likely describing the language itself. Translating each word: 'Zduvvu' closely maps to 'stuffy' (s->z, t->d, ff->vv, y->u). 'Dulg' maps to 'talk' (t->d, a->u, l->l, k->g). 'Duzu' is very close to 'dizzy' (d->d, i->u, zz->zz, y->u -> duzzu), a fitting description for an ambiguous language. The resulting phrase is 'Stuffy Dizzy Talk'.
